skill.name: distributed-tracing
skill.description: Implement distributed tracing with Jaeger, Tempo, and OpenTelemetry for microservice request visibility and latency analysis.
skill.summary: Guide OpenTelemetry tracing setup for Jaeger and Tempo across distributed services.
skill.author: wshobson

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported 53 issues, but the weak-cryptography, dynamic-import, and shell-backtick alerts are false positives from Markdown front matter, Go import syntax, code fences, and inline references. Confirmed risk is medium because users may copy Kubernetes deployment commands and tracing examples that contact remote or internal endpoints and may store sensitive trace metadata. No evidence of malware, prompt injection, credential exfiltration, or hidden executable code was found.

medium_findings.title: Copyable Kubernetes Deployment Commands
medium_findings.description: The shell example creates a namespace, fetches a Jaeger operator manifest from GitHub, and applies a Jaeger custom resource. This is legitimate observability guidance, but copying it into a production cluster can modify infrastructure and trust a remote manifest without review.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The deployment commands are directly present in a Markdown bash block. They appear legitimate for the skill purpose, so the risk is operational rather than malicious.

medium_findings.title: Trace Metadata May Expose Sensitive Data
medium_findings.description: The examples show recording database statements and recommend meaningful tags such as user_id. Trace backends can retain and expose these values, so users should redact sensitive attributes before production use.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The skill explicitly demonstrates db.statement and user_id-style trace attributes. This is common observability practice, but it creates a real privacy risk if values are not sanitized.

low_findings.title: Dynamic Import Alert Is a False Positive
low_findings.description: The dynamic import finding points to a Go import list inside a Markdown code example. It is not JavaScript dynamic import syntax and is not executed by the skill.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The referenced lines are clearly a Go import block within fenced Markdown. There is no runtime loader or user-controlled import behavior.

low_findings.title: Backtick Execution Alerts Are Markdown Fences
low_findings.description: The Ruby or shell backtick findings are caused by Markdown code fences and inline reference paths. No evidence found of shell backtick execution in runnable skill code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged syntax appears in documentation formatting and code examples. The skill contains only SKILL.md, so these snippets are not automatically executed.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Alert Is a False Positive
low_findings.description: The weak cryptographic algorithm finding points to the YAML description line. No cryptographic algorithm or hashing implementation appears at that location.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line 3 is plain descriptive metadata about distributed tracing. The scanner likely matched text accidentally, and there is no crypto usage to evaluate there.

dangerous_patterns.title: Remote Manifest Applied to Kubernetes
dangerous_patterns.description: The example uses kubectl to create resources from a remote GitHub URL. Users should pin and review manifests before applying them to shared clusters.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The command is explicit and copyable. It is not malicious, but it has supply-chain and infrastructure-change implications.

dangerous_patterns.title: HTTP Collector and Service Endpoints
dangerous_patterns.description: Several examples use HTTP URLs for Jaeger collectors, Elasticsearch, and downstream services. These may be internal examples, but production deployments should use authenticated and encrypted transport where possible.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The hardcoded URLs are visible in examples and match the skill domain. The concern depends on deployment context, so confidence is moderate.

content.value_statement: Distributed services make latency and failures hard to trace across request paths. This skill guides OpenTelemetry, Jaeger, and Tempo setup so teams can inspect spans, dependencies, and bottlenecks.
actual_capabilities: Explains trace, span, context, tag, and log concepts for distributed systems.
Provides Jaeger setup examples for Kubernetes and Docker Compose.
Shows OpenTelemetry instrumentation patterns for Python, Node.js, and Go services.
Demonstrates HTTP trace context propagation with Python requests and Node.js axios.
Covers Tempo configuration, sampling strategies, trace analysis queries, and log correlation.
limitations: It is a guidance-only Markdown skill and does not run deployment automation by itself.
Examples use placeholder service names, endpoints, buckets, and simplified application logic.
It does not include full security hardening for production collectors, storage, or ingress.
Reference files mentioned in the text are not present in the reviewed package.

use_cases.title: Debug Microservice Latency
use_cases.description: Trace slow requests across frontend, gateway, service, and database spans to locate bottlenecks.

use_cases.title: Instrument New Services
use_cases.description: Add OpenTelemetry tracing to Python, Node.js, or Go services with consistent service names and spans.

use_cases.title: Plan Observability Rollout
use_cases.description: Compare Jaeger and Tempo setup patterns, sampling choices, and trace storage considerations before deployment.

prompt_templates.title: Start a Basic Tracing Plan
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner setup for a small service group
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me design a basic distributed tracing plan for three microservices using OpenTelemetry and Jaeger. Include service names, first spans, and a safe sampling rate.

prompt_templates.title: Instrument an Existing Service
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my service flow and suggest OpenTelemetry spans, attributes, and context propagation points. Avoid collecting secrets or personal data.

prompt_templates.title: Troubleshoot Missing Traces
prompt_templates.prompt: Traces are not appearing in Jaeger. Walk me through endpoint checks, sampling checks, context propagation checks, and application log checks.

prompt_templates.title: Design Production Trace Governance
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a production tracing policy for Kubernetes services using Tempo. Cover sampling, retention, attribute redaction, transport security, and alerting.

output_examples.input: Find why checkout requests are slow across five services.
output_examples.output: A trace analysis plan that compares span durations, highlights gateway and database segments, and lists likely bottlenecks to validate.

output_examples.input: Add tracing to a new API service.
output_examples.output: A practical instrumentation checklist covering tracer setup, route spans, downstream context propagation, error recording, and safe attribute naming.
A deployment review covering storage, sampling, retention, collector endpoints, security controls, and dashboard integration.

best_practices: Review and pin remote manifests before applying Kubernetes examples to any shared cluster.
Redact user identifiers, database statements, tokens, and request bodies before exporting spans.
Set sampling, retention, and access controls before enabling tracing in production.
anti_patterns: Do not copy placeholder endpoints, buckets, or service names directly into production.
Do not trace every request at full volume without measuring cost and latency overhead.
Do not store sensitive attributes in Jaeger, Tempo, logs, or trace search indexes.

faq.question: Does this skill deploy Jaeger or Tempo automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides examples and guidance that a user must review and run manually.

faq.question: Which languages are covered?
faq.answer: The skill includes OpenTelemetry examples for Python Flask, Node.js Express, and Go.

faq.question: Can it help with trace context propagation?
faq.answer: Yes. It shows HTTP header propagation patterns for Python requests and Node.js axios.

faq.question: Is it safe for production clusters?
faq.answer: It can inform production work, but all manifests, endpoints, storage, and security settings need review first.

faq.question: Does it cover sampling?
faq.answer: Yes. It explains probabilistic, rate limiting, and adaptive sampling patterns.

faq.question: What should users protect when tracing?
faq.answer: Users should avoid exporting secrets, personal data, full SQL statements, request bodies, and sensitive identifiers.
